text: This study examines the moral dilemmas emanating from the implementation of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in higher institutions of learning in Africa, while making reference to the socio-technical context of the continent. While AI in higher institutions of learning in Africa is revolutionising the academic sector, it also raises a number of ethical issues. Drawing on a desk-based research methodology, that synthesised information from journal articles, research articles and institutional reports among others, this study identifies the ethico-moral concerns emanating from the use of AI, most of which have been reported in existing literature. The ethical concerns that were revealed emanated from three broad sources of concern namely data privacy issues, lack of accountability due to the opacity of the AI system as well as issues of discrimination in as far as the students and staff are concerned, emanating from bias in the decisions made by the technology. In order to make the use of AI in higher learning institutions in Africa a success, the stakeholders in the sector have to ensure that they adhere to the tenets of fairness, equity and justice and also ensure that the rights of the students and staff are guaranteed, while ensuring that the technology is also efficient. This study therefore recommends that, any future studies conducted in this area should be anchored on the principles of critical theory and ethics of care that will ensure the humanity of the technology. In addition to that, the study recommends that ethics education concerning the use of AI should be taught in the institutions and that policies should be put in place that ensures that the technological innovation is implemented in a manner that reflects the African values and ethics. The policies will have to entail, among others, issues of accountability as far as the technology is concerned, ensuring transparency of the working of the algorithm, as well as rules and regulations that are aimed at ensuring proper use of data, amongst others.
explanation: The text relies on broad, generic academic phrasing and lacks specific empirical detail, a pattern commonly seen in AI-generated outputs. It could also be human-written, but the generic structure makes AI-origin likely.
